metadata.title: What Good Looks Like: Your Phase Gates
metadata.playlist: Say What It Is — The Conductor Brief
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 5 brief — The Conductor Brief: Part 4 (12 pts)

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, Episode 4 of 7. Part 4 is the 'good looks like' half of your phase gates — twelve points. Episode 5 is the 'bad looks like' half. Together they are 24 points. Here is why they are separate: beginners write vague criteria when the two get blurred. When you try to write good and bad in one pass, you end up with criteria like 'the output is correct' — which is neither checkable nor actionable. Part 4 forces one question at a time: at each step in your pipeline, what does a correct, trustworthy output actually look like? Specifically enough to make a yes-or-no call. A phase gate is a human checkpoint — a moment where you stop, look at what came out of the previous step, and decide whether to proceed or not. Part 4 asks you to write the 'proceed' criteria for at least three steps: data ingestion, validation, AI processing, and final output.

props.segment: What Good Looks Like: Your Phase Gates
props.command: Write 'good looks like' criteria for at least 3 pipeline steps — specific enough to make a yes/no call at each gate.
props.runningText: Part 4 is the 'proceed' signal at each gate — Part 5 is the 'stop' signal — keep them separate…
output: Phase gate = human checkpoint: stop, look at output, proceed or not
4 steps: data ingestion · validation/GIGO · AI processing · final output
Specific enough to be a yes/no call — not 'the output looks right'

beats.narration_text: Gate 1 is data ingestion. What does good look like when raw data arrives? Write the criteria for what must be true about the data before your pipeline processes it. Specific examples: 'The CSV file exists at data/raw/source.csv, has at least 500 rows, and every row has a non-null value in the text and date columns.' 'The NewsAPI response returns at least 50 articles, each with a non-null title, description, and publishedAt field.' 'The YouTube API response contains at least 20 video records with non-null videoId and statistics fields.' The pattern is the same for every source: the file or response exists, it has a minimum record count, and the required fields are present and non-null. That is what good looks like at ingestion. If any of those criteria fail, you do not proceed to validation — you stop and investigate why the source did not deliver. The ingestion gate tells you the data arrived. It does not tell you the data is any good — that is Gate 2.

props.artifactHeading: What good looks like: the data arrived
artifactLines: 'CSV exists at data/raw/source.csv · at least 500 rows · text + date columns non-null.'
'NewsAPI response returns ≥50 articles · each with non-null title, description, publishedAt.'
'YouTube API: ≥20 records · non-null videoId + statistics fields.'
Pattern: file/response exists + minimum record count + required fields present and non-null.
Ingestion gate = the data arrived. Not: the data is good. That is Gate 2.
If Gate 1 fails: stop, do not proceed to validation, investigate the source.
props.sparkLine: The data arrived. Required fields present. Minimum count met.

beats.narration_text: Gate 2 is validation — the GIGO gate. Garbage in, garbage out. This is the most important gate because it decides what your tool builds on. What does good look like at validation? The data meets the quality standard you wrote in Part 3. The criteria should be specific enough that you could check them with a script. 'No column has more than 5 percent null values.' 'Text fields average at least 20 words.' 'All date fields parse as valid ISO 8601 dates.' 'No duplicate records as measured by the unique ID column.' The validation gate does not fix the data — it is a yes-or-no decision. Either the data meets the standard and you proceed to processing, or it does not and you stop. Notice the connection back to Part 3: the quality standard you wrote in the data contract is exactly the condition your Gate 2 checks. If you wrote a real, checkable standard in Part 3, Gate 2 almost writes itself.

props.artifactHeading: What good looks like: the data meets the standard
artifactLines: Gate 2 checks the Part 3 quality standard. If you wrote a checkable standard, this gate writes itself.
'No column >5% null values.' 'Text fields average ≥20 words.' 'Dates parse as valid ISO 8601.'
'No duplicate records as measured by the unique ID column.'
Validation gate = yes/no decision. Does not fix data — decides whether to proceed.
If Gate 2 fails: stop. Do not pass garbage to the AI processing step.
GIGO means garbage has a definition. Gate 2 applies that definition.
props.sparkLine: Gate 2 checks Part 3's quality standard. No standard → no gate.

beats.narration_text: Gate 3 is AI processing. What does good look like when your tool generates its output? This gate is harder to write because AI outputs vary. The strategy is to name the properties of a trustworthy output, not a perfect one. 'The output contains a summary for each input record.' 'Each summary is between 50 and 200 words.' 'The sentiment field contains one of: positive, negative, neutral.' 'The output JSON is valid and parseable.' These are structural criteria — shape and completeness, not accuracy. You cannot check accuracy at Gate 3 unless you have ground truth to compare against. What you can check is structure: did the model return something for every input, is the response in the expected format, are the required fields present? Write criteria that you can verify automatically — because at scale, you will not be reading every output by hand.

props.artifactHeading: What good looks like: trustworthy output structure
artifactLines: 'Output contains a summary for each input record.' (completeness)
'Each summary is 50–200 words.' (format bounds)
'Sentiment field contains exactly one of: positive, negative, neutral.' (enum constraint)
'Output JSON is valid and parseable.' (structural integrity)
Gate 3 checks shape and completeness — not accuracy. You cannot check accuracy without ground truth.
Write criteria you can verify automatically — at scale you will not read every output by hand.
props.sparkLine: Shape and completeness. Not accuracy — you need ground truth for that.

beats.act: gate 4 — final output and the coherence test
beats.narration_text: Gate 4 is the final output — what the user actually sees. What does good look like for a non-technical user? 'A brief with five bullets and no raw data.' 'A table with exactly three columns: topic, sentiment score, and recommended action.' 'A PDF report that opens in any browser and has no broken links.' 'The report loads in under three seconds on a standard laptop.' This gate is the user-facing check. The criteria are readable, not technical. If a non-technical stakeholder looked at this output, would they understand it and be able to act on it? That is the question. Connect this gate back to your Part 2A user: the person you named in the user profile — in their situation, with their level of technical knowledge — would they look at this output and know what to do next? If yes, the gate passes. The coherence test for Part 4: every gate's 'good looks like' should be traceable to what your Part 1 sentence promises the user.

props.artifactHeading: What good looks like: a non-technical user can act on it
artifactLines: '5-bullet brief with no raw data.' 'Table with 3 columns: topic / sentiment / recommended action.'
'PDF loads in under 3 seconds. No broken links. Opens in any browser.'
Test: the Part 2A user — in their situation, with their level of technical knowledge — looks at this and knows what to do next.
If yes: gate passes. If they ask 'what do I do with this?' — the output isn't done.
Coherence test: every gate's 'good looks like' should trace back to what Part 1 promises.
If Part 1 says the user can 'decide X' — Gate 4 must produce the thing they need to make that decision.
props.sparkLine: The Part 2A user looks at this and knows what to do next.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Part 4. A phase gate is a human checkpoint: a yes-or-no decision about whether to proceed. 'Good looks like' is the proceed criteria. Write it for at least three steps: data ingestion — file exists, minimum record count, required fields present; validation — the Part 3 quality standard passes; AI processing — output is complete and structurally correct; final output — a non-technical user can act on it. Trap: vague criteria you can't act on. 'The output looks right' is not a gate. Write each criterion as a checkable condition. The connection to Part 3: Gate 2 directly applies the quality standards you wrote there. If those standards were vague, your Gate 2 will be too.

props.artifactTitle: What Good Looks Like: Your Phase Gates
artifactLines: Phase gate = yes/no checkpoint: does the output meet the proceed criteria?
Gate 1 — Ingestion: file exists · minimum record count · required fields non-null.
Gate 2 — Validation: Part 3 quality standards pass. No standard → no gate.
Gate 3 — AI processing: output complete and structurally correct (shape, not accuracy).
Gate 4 — Final output: Part 2A user can look at this and know what to do next.
Trap: vague criteria. 'The output looks right' is not a phase gate.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this prompt into Claude. Fill in the brackets before running. The prompt reads: Here is my tool's pipeline: [paste a 3-to-5 step description of what your n8n workflow does — ingestion to final output]. Here is my Part 3 quality standard: [paste it]. Here is my Part 2A user: [paste the user profile]. Write Part 4 of the Conductor Brief — 'good looks like' criteria for each pipeline step. For each step: name the step, write a 'good looks like' statement that is specific enough to make a yes-or-no proceed decision, and note whether the criterion is checkable automatically or requires human review. Use the Part 3 quality standard directly in the validation step — do not rewrite it, just reference it. For the final output step, write the criterion from the Part 2A user's perspective: they look at the output and know what to do next. After the prompt runs: are all criteria checkable? Does Gate 4 trace back to what Part 1 promises? If not, revise before moving to Part 5.

props.segment: What Good Looks Like: Your Phase Gates
props.command: Here is my pipeline: [paste 3–5 step description]. Here is my Part 3 quality standard: [paste it]. Here is my Part 2A user: [paste it]. Write Part 4 — 'good looks like' for each step: specific enough to make a yes/no proceed decision. Use the Part 3 standard directly in the validation step. For the final output step, write it from the Part 2A user's perspective: they look at this and know what to do next.
props.runningText: paste your pipeline, Part 3 standard, and Part 2A user before running — gates are specific to your steps…

beats.narration_text: What Good Looks Like: Your Phase Gates. Liam, in for Bear.
